data.title: Federal Realty raises 2026 core FFO guidance to $7.46-$7.55 as leasing and recycling accelerate
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Federal Realty Investment Trust (FRT) Q1 2026

Management View

  • CEO Donald Wood said the quarter reflected “stepped-up capital recycling portfolio-wide,” “near record leasing,” and “meaningful incremental contributions from previous year's development spend,” resulting in “FFO per share of $1.88” and “enabling us to raise guidance for the balance of the year.”
  • Wood highlighted portfolio actions and balance-sheet accretive recycling, saying the company sold Misora apartments at Santana Row and Courthouse Shopping Center for “combined proceeds of $159 million,” then acquired Congressional North Shopping Center for “$72 million at a 7% stabilized yield,” adding that “opportunities for additional accretive acquisitions net of dispositions continue to be a laser-like focus.”
  • Wood emphasized operating strength and leasing, stating the portfolio ended “96.1% leased and 93.8% occupied,” and that the quarter included “over 100 leases and 649,000 feet of comparable deals… at 13% cash rollover.”
  • Wood pointed to mixed-use office leasing strength, saying “Santana West is officially 100% leased,” “all of Santana Row's office space is 100% leased,” and “whole office portfolio, 99% overall leased,” while adding the company would not pursue speculative office starts: “It would only happen to the extent we have a build-to-suit… I'm not going to spec.”
  • COO Wendy Seher said, “This was a strong quarter across the board,” citing “Comparable POI growth… at 4.7%,” “Foot traffic was up 3% for the quarter, and more importantly, 4% in April,” and “Executed but not yet occupied deals will contribute an incremental $36 million of rent over the balance of the year and into 2027.”
  • CFO Daniel Guglielmone said, “Our FFO per share of $1.88 for the first quarter reflects almost a 11% growth versus last year,” and attributed drivers including “higher revenues through better occupancy, parking revenues and ancillary income,” “expense savings,” “higher-than-forecast term fees,” and “timing, pulling forward some items that were expected later in the year.”

Outlook

  • Guglielmone raised full-year guidance: “We are raising guidance for both NAREIT and core FFO to $7.46 to $7.55 per share,” and said the midpoint increase “represents 6.3% growth for core FFO when compared to 2025.”
  • He lifted the comparable POI outlook to “3.125% to 3.625% from the previous range of 3% to 3.5%,” and kept the credit reserve “flat at 60 to 85 basis points of rental income.”
  • He provided quarterly cadence expectations: “The second quarter is $1.83 to $1.86. The third quarter is $1.84 to $1.87 with the fourth quarter in the low to mid-$1.90s per share.”
  • He described balance-sheet and interest-rate assumptions following refinancing activity, noting the company “refinanced our 1.25% unsecured notes… to assume roughly 4.5% of the effective interest rate reset.”

Financial Results

  • Guglielmone said FFO per share of $1.88 was “$0.06 plus, or 3.6% above the midpoint of our guidance range,” and detailed quarter drivers including “$0.02 from higher revenues,” “$0.01 from expense savings,” “$0.01 from higher-than-forecast term fees” and “$0.02 attributed to timing.”
  • He reported “Cash basis comparable growth was 5.1% for the quarter,” and said, “Excluding term fees, the result was still roughly 4%,” while adding “Cash basis min rent increased 3.6% for the quarter.”
  • On liquidity and leverage, he cited an upsized revolver to “$1.4 billion,” and said “First quarter annualized net debt to EBITDA is 5.5x” and “Fixed charge coverage is 3.9x.”
  • On capital recycling, he said the quarter included “asset sales of $159 million… at a blended mid-4s cap rate,” with “an additional $66 million of sales in process… with cap rates targeted in the mid- to upper 5% range.”

Q&A

  • Samir Khanal, BofA: asked how the K-shaped economy affects relative strength; CEO Donald Wood said “we operate in the top part of the K” and cited “household income of $167,000 overall” and “$11 billion per shopping center of purchasing power.”
  • Michael Goldsmith, UBS: asked what “inning” capital recycling is in; CEO Wood said it is “continuously forever,” and CFO Daniel Guglielmone added that “20% to 25% of the overall FFO growth this year was driven by acquisitions.”
  • Juan Sanabria, BMO: asked about same-store trajectory and occupancy cadence; CFO Guglielmone said occupancy stays “in the mid- to upper 93% range” before moving to “mid- to upper 94% range by year-end,” with comparable growth dipping “closer to 2” then returning “into kind of the 3.5% to 4% range.”
  • Cooper Clark, Wells Fargo: asked about multifamily disposition pipeline and cap rates; CEO Wood said “I don't have any particular residential property on the market,” and tied timing to acquisition matching and “1031,” while CIO Jan Sweetnam said, “we are seeing a lot more opportunities today than we were just 3 months ago.”
  • Greg McGinniss, Scotiabank: asked about starting ground-up office development; CEO Wood said, “It would only happen to the extent we have a build-to-suit… I'm not going to spec.”
  • Craig Mailman, Citi: asked to bridge the FFO cadence; CFO Guglielmone cited a “refinancing headwind,” early multifamily lease-up drag at The Blair, and “timing-related things” pulled into Q1.
  • Haendel St. Juste, Mizuho: asked about sustainability of earnings trajectory and green bond refinancing; CEO Wood deferred earnings trajectory to Investor Day, and CFO Guglielmone said, “We will be opportunistic in either hitting the bond market or the convert market.”
  • Floris Van Dijkum, Ladenburg: asked about Assembly Row; CEO Wood said life science “took a back seat,” but the company is entitling “3 million… 4 million square feet,” describing “value banking.”
  • Follow-ups: CFO Guglielmone said pulled-forward items were “some FAS 141 benefits,” CEO Wood attributed elevated reimbursements to “snow removal and utility expense,” and CFO Guglielmone responded to a guidance-revision prompt: “I don't think I referred to a… positive revision going forward.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst sentiment was slightly positive, with multiple questions pressing on sustainability, cadence, dispositions, and development risk (e.g., asking to “bridge the $1.88” to the next quarters and whether the company would “start more ground-up office development”).
  • Management sentiment was positive in prepared remarks and generally steady in Q&A, with confidence language such as CEO Wood saying Q1 “increased my confidence,” while also drawing boundaries on risk (e.g., “I'm not going to spec”).
  • Compared with Q4 2025, management messaging remained confident on leasing and recycling, but Q1 included more explicit discussion of near-term “timing” and cadence headwinds (refinancing and early residential lease-up) alongside a raised full-year range.

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Guidance moved higher quarter-over-quarter: Q4 2025 guidance was “$7.42 to $7.52 per share,” while Q1 2026 guidance rose to “$7.46 to $7.55 per share.”
  • Q4 emphasized the introduction of “Core FFO” and a Saks-related “noncash charge,” while Q1 emphasized outperformance drivers and timing items, including “FAS 141 benefits” pulled forward.
  • Capital recycling remained central in both quarters, but Q1 added detail on a completed adjacent acquisition (Congressional North) and described market supply as having “clearly picked up.”
  • Analyst focus shifted from Core FFO framing and tenant credit/bankruptcy impacts in Q4 toward cadence, acquisitions competitiveness, and the sustainability of outperformance in Q1.

Risks and Concerns

  • CFO Guglielmone cited a refinancing drag, stating the company “assume[s] roughly 4.5% of the effective interest rate reset,” calling it a “refinancing headwind.”
  • Management highlighted weather-driven cost pressure, with CEO Wood citing “higher snow removal and related energy expenses,” and later attributing reimbursements to “a huge amount of weather impacts in the Northeast.”
  • On development risk discipline, CEO Wood said new office starts would require a build-to-suit structure: “I'm not going to spec.”

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q1 2026 as an operational and capital-recycling inflection, highlighting $1.88 FFO per share, record leasing volume, and expanding signed-not-occupied rent commencements, while raising full-year NAREIT and core FFO guidance to $7.46-$7.55 and laying out a cadence that includes near-term refinancing and early residential lease-up drag before contractual occupancy growth lifts results later in 2026.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Boston Beer slumps after weak demand impacts Q1 results
data.description:
Boston Beer (SAM) slumped on Friday after disclosing soft Q1 results due to demand pressure that led to depletions being down 4%, shipments down 6.9%, and revenue falling 4.4% to $433.9M. Non-GAAP EPS came in at $1.64 to miss the consensus mark by $0.33. 

On the positive side, gross margin improved to 49.3% in Q1 despite the volume decline due to procurement savings and brewery efficiencies. Boston Beer (SAM) also tightened its full-year adjusted EPS guidance to $8.50 to $10.50 (midpoint $9.50) vs. $9.89 consensus.

Weighing in on the report, UBS analyst Peter Grom said that the beer seller acknowledged incremental cost pressures related to aluminum/freight and observed management seemed to emphasize that this would be manageable as the company leans further into procurement savings, brewery efficiencies, and network optimization. "We are encouraged by the margin performance/outlook but think the catalyst path from here hinges on whether top-line trends can improve. And with valuation more or less in line with historical averages, we think the risk/reward is balanced and would wait until there are greater signs that an inflection is taking shape before becoming constructive," warned Grom.

Shares of Boston Beer (SAM) were down 10.7% in Friday afternoon trading.

data.title: Forum Energy Technologies forecasts Q2 EBITDA of $24M-$30M while raising full-year EBITDA midpoint to $103M
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Forum Energy Technologies (FET) Q1 2026

Management view

  • "Our first quarter results reinforced our confidence in the path we presented with FET 2030." (President, CEO & Director Neal Lux) "Year-over-year, we increased revenue 8%, EBITDA 14% and net income 300%... Orders were up 10% year-over-year with a book-to-bill of 106%... Compared to the first quarter of last year, our backlog is up 44%... These efforts have achieved $15 million of annualized savings." (President, CEO & Director Lux)

  • "We are forecasting second quarter EBITDA of between $24 million and $30 million" and "we are raising the midpoint of our EBITDA guidance to $103 million." (President, CEO & Director Lux) "Importantly, while we are seeing signs of increased activity... our forecast conservatively assumes a flat market." (President, CEO & Director Lux)

  • "First quarter revenue of $209 million came in near the top end of our guidance" and "Adjusted EBITDA for the quarter was $23 million, in line with our guidance." (Executive VP & CFO David Williams) "Consolidated free cash flow was $1 million, consistent with our guidance." (Executive VP & CFO Williams)

  • "Duracoil-95... is continuing to gain traction and is now active on 3 continents" and "we received a significant order for multiple systems to be deployed in Argentina this year" for DuraLine. (President, CEO & Director Lux) "We have developed patent-pending software for the FR120 iron roughneck... and increases drilling efficiency by 30%." (President, CEO & Director Lux)

Outlook

  • "We are forecasting revenue between $200 million and $225 million and EBITDA between $24 million to $30 million" for Q2, with "Adjusted net income expected for the second quarter... between $6 million and $11 million." (Executive VP & CFO Williams)

  • "We are raising the bottom end of our EBITDA guidance range from $90 million to $95 million" while "maintaining our revenue guidance of $800 million to $880 million." (Executive VP & CFO Williams) "For adjusted net income, we are guiding between $21 million and $38 million" and "we reaffirm our full year free cash flow guidance of $55 million to $75 million." (Executive VP & CFO Williams)

  • Change vs. prior quarter guidance language centered on EBITDA range tightening: "For full year 2026, we are guiding... EBITDA of $90 million to $110 million" (President, CEO & Director Lux; Q4 call) compared with "raising the bottom end... from $90 million to $95 million" and "raising the midpoint... to $103 million" (Executive VP & CFO Williams; President, CEO & Director Lux; Q1 call).

Financial results

  • "First quarter revenue of $209 million" was driven as "our international revenue was up 7%" and "offshore revenue expanded 10%, driven by a 20% increase in our Subsea product line." (Executive VP & CFO Williams)

  • Segment detail included "Drilling and Completions revenue was $127 million" and "Artificial lift and downhole revenue was $82 million." (Executive VP & CFO Williams) "Coiled tubing revenue was down 17%... due to customer requested delivery pushouts into the second quarter." (Executive VP & CFO Williams)

  • Balance sheet and capital returns updates included: "We repurchased almost 93,000 shares for approximately $5 million" and "We ended the quarter with net debt of $121 million, with a net leverage ratio... under 1.4x." (Executive VP & CFO Williams) "We extended our credit facility maturity to February 2031 with improved pricing" and "Liquidity of $91 million remains strong with $54 million available under our revolving credit facility." (Executive VP & CFO Williams)

Q&A

  • Jeffrey Robertson, Water Tower Research LLC: "are you seeing demand for [Unity] outside of traditional energy?" and "are there orders... within... the backlog?" President, CEO & Director Lux: "I do think it would have an application outside oil and gas" and "we do have a number of Unity systems already in the backlog."

  • Jeffrey Robertson, Water Tower Research LLC: "what the margin profile looks like in the backlog?" President, CEO & Director Lux: "our innovative products have higher margins" and "about 11% was new innovations." Executive VP & CFO Williams: "Typically, we see our subsea business with slightly lower contribution margins... that tends to pull the average margin down."

  • Steve Ferazani, Sidoti & Company, LLC: on the guidance raise and orders strength; President, CEO & Director Lux: "having a book-to-bill over 100% does give you a lot of confidence" and "we are getting initial... indications of some increase in activity. It's uneven so far."

  • Steve Ferazani, Sidoti & Company, LLC: on Q1 cash flow timing; Executive VP & CFO Williams: "The seasonality is driven by incentive comp payments and property tax payments that go out in Q1" and "we think that will unwind in the second and third quarters."

  • Daniel Pickering, Pickering Energy Partners LP: on FET 2030 math; President, CEO & Director Lux: "we see, let's call it, 30% incremental margins" and "let's call it around 20% EBITDA margins" as operating leverage scales internationally.

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts: slightly negative-to-neutral pressure on visibility and cash flow timing, including "we're certainly surprised by the strength in the order book" (Steve Ferazani, Sidoti & Company, LLC) and detailed probing on buybacks vs. M&A and valuation expectations.

  • Management: slightly positive in prepared remarks and more explanatory in Q&A, with confidence language such as "our results should increase substantially" (Executive VP & CFO Williams) and conditional upside framing: "Should the market pick up, I would expect to see further upside" (President, CEO & Director Lux).

  • Versus last quarter, the current call added more near-term momentum language ("providing momentum into the second quarter") (President, CEO & Director Lux), while the prior call emphasized navigating headwinds: "Despite a challenging backdrop... our teams executed with discipline and focus." (President, CEO & Director Lux; Q4 call)

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Guidance progression shifted from establishing the year framework to tightening: "we are guiding revenue between $800 million and $880 million and EBITDA of $90 million to $110 million" (President, CEO & Director Lux; Q4 call) versus "raising the bottom end of our EBITDA guidance range from $90 million to $95 million" while "maintaining... revenue guidance" (Executive VP & CFO Williams; Q1 call).

  • Product narrative evolved from launches to commercialization traction: Q4 highlighted "we commercialized 10 new products" (President, CEO & Director Lux; Q4 call), while Q1 emphasized adoption and orders, including Duracoil-95 "active on 3 continents" and DuraLine receiving "a significant order" for Argentina. (President, CEO & Director Lux)

  • Analyst focus rotated from subsea trajectory and working capital sustainability (Q4) to backlog margin mix, Middle East disruption exposure, and non-oil-and-gas demand potential for Unity and data center cooling (Q1).

Risks and concerns

  • Geopolitical/logistics risk was acknowledged: "We have experienced some disruptions... particularly around logistics and freight costs" tied to the Middle East conflict, while "we are not forecasting any material negative impact" and "Middle East revenue is only 10% of our total." (President, CEO & Director Lux)

  • Execution and mix risk appeared in margin discussions: "cost savings benefits were largely offset by product mix" (Executive VP & CFO Williams) and subsea backlog was described as having "slightly lower contribution margins" due to "pass-through material and electronics." (Executive VP & CFO Williams)

Final takeaway

Management presented Q1 as a setup quarter for higher Q2 profitability, anchored by backlog conversion, completed structural cost actions, and market share gains from recently commercialized products. The company raised the lower end of full-year EBITDA guidance while keeping revenue guidance unchanged, reiterated its free cash flow conversion target, and framed geopolitical disruption as manageable given limited Middle East revenue exposure, while continuing to prioritize share repurchases alongside selective, per-share-accretive M&A.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Most and least shorted energy stocks with over $2B market cap as of April
data.description:
In April, the S&P 500 energy sector (XLE) moved 1.2% in the upward direction this year, supported by a 0.8% rise in crude oil futures (CL1:COM). 

Against this backdrop, short interest trends among mid, large and mega-cap energy stocks show a wide divergence. Centrus Energy (LEU) was the most shorted stock in April, while Enterprise Products Partners (EPD) was the least shorted stock.

Most shorted energy stocks as of April end:

  • Centrus Energy (LEU) 22.73%
  • Solaris Energy Infrastructure (SEI) 22.36%
  • Sable Offshore (SOC) 19.18%
  • Transocean (RIG) 17.62%
  • Northern Oil and Gas (NOG) 16.72%

Least shorted energy stocks as of April end:

  • Enterprise Products Partners (EPD) 0.78%
  • MPLX (MPLX) 0.80%
  • Energy Transfer (ET) 0.84%
  • Chevron (CVX) 1.01%
  • Exxon Mobil (XOM) 1.03%

Energy ETFs: (XLE), (AMLP), (VDE), (XOP), (OIH), and (IXC).More on State Street® Energy Select Sector SPDR® ETF

data.title: Cboe expects $100M-$120M annualized expense savings as it lowers 2026 adjusted operating expense guide to $838M-$853M
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Cboe Global Markets (CBOE) Q1 2026

Management view

  • "Cboe delivered another quarter of record net revenue and adjusted earnings powered by continued strength across all of our core businesses," said CEO Craig Donohue. "During the first quarter, Cboe grew net revenue 29% year-over-year to a record $729 million, and adjusted diluted EPS increased an exceptional 48% to a record $3.70."

  • On derivatives, Donohue said, "Index options net transaction and clearing fees revenue drove the upside, increasing a robust 35% as our proprietary SPX options set another quarterly record with average daily volume increasing 34% year-over-year to 4.9 million contracts." He added that March dynamics shifted, with "0DTE options still grew, but at a more steady 6% rate month-over-month in March, while non-0DTE options jumped over 26%, helping to drive a new monthly ADV record of 5.4 million SPX options contracts."

  • On new products, Donohue said, "Subject to regulatory approval, we plan to bring our securities-based event contracts to market," describing a structure "designed to mirror the risk/reward profile of a widely used options strategy, the vertical call spread." He also framed expansion plans: "we intend to expand beyond index-based outcomes by leveraging our capabilities across both securities and futures" and "introduce additional contracts around economic and financial indicators."

  • COO Scott Johnston said the company’s strategic realignment and portfolio actions "are expected to reduce our workforce by approximately 20%." He added, "we will also be transitioning back to in-person work to support faster decision-making, stronger collaboration and better integration across teams."

  • "Adjusted operating expenses of $201 million were up 4% on a year-over-year basis," said CFO Jill Griebenow. "Adjusted operating EBITDA of $541 million grew 41% and adjusted operating EBITDA margin expanded by 6.1 percentage points to 74.2%."

Outlook

  • "On a full year basis, we anticipate our Data Vantage organic net revenue growth to be in the low-double-digit range," Griebenow said, adding, "We expect our total organic net revenue growth to be in the low-double-digit to mid-teens range."

  • Griebenow said, "We are lowering our 2026 adjusted operating expense guidance range from $864 million to $879 million to $838 million to $853 million." She also reiterated, "Our full year guidance range for CapEx remains $73 million to $83 million, and depreciation and amortization remains in the $56 million to $60 million range," and "we continue to expect the effective tax rate on adjusted earnings under the current tax laws to come in at 27.5% to 29.5% for the full year."

  • On strategic realignment financial targets, Griebenow said, "we now expect our strategic realignment to deliver an even greater reduction, approximately 12% to 14% on an annualized basis compared to 2025, translating to savings in the range of $100 million to $120 million," and "we anticipate realizing $20 million to $25 million of the additional strategic realignment savings in 2026."

Financial results

  • Adjusted diluted EPS was $3.70 vs. $3.37 (analysts’ estimate), and net revenue was $729 million vs. $708,607,170.0 (analysts’ estimate).

  • Griebenow said segment performance was broad-based: "The Options segment delivered another quarter of record net revenue, increasing 33% year-over-year" and "Total options ADV was up 10%, with a 29% increase in index options volume and a 4% increase in multi-listed options volume." She added, "North American Equities net revenue rose 18%" and "Europe and APAC produced 32% year-over-year net revenue growth," while "Futures net revenue increased 9%" and "global FX produced the strongest net revenue growth of our segment, up 38% on a year-over-year basis."

  • On Data Vantage drivers, Griebenow said, "net revenues increased by 19%" and "healthy new subscription and unit sales, representing approximately 85% of this quarter's growth, with the remainder coming from pricing changes."

Q&A

  • Patrick Moley, Piper Sandler: Asked about Data Vantage sustainability and whether low-double-digit growth becomes a baseline; Management response: "we did see very robust demand" and noted "one-time revenue related to historic data sets" tied to "2 new products related to options data sets," adding, "we are taking our guidance up this quarter to low-double digits for the year" but "I wouldn't say that's a new baseline."

  • Brian Bedell, Deutsche Bank: Asked how Cboe envisions prediction markets, including KPI-based contracts and structure; CEO Craig Donohue: "we're super keen on coming to market with company-specific contracts" and "long term, we certainly see the opportunity also to expand into CFTC regulated futures or swaps"; Executive VP Robert Hocking: "exploring what KPI-based contracts would look like... really leading more on the securities side" with "clear resolution kind of disclosure-based settlement" and "decades of surveillance controls."

  • Elias Abboud, BofA Securities: Asked about competitive defensibility of SPX and ecosystem depth; Executive VP Robert Hocking detailed a participation mix including "about 84% electronic, 16% open outcry" and said "34 different retail broker platforms connected in trading," with volume concentration where "the largest representing only about 30% of the volume."

  • Daniel Fannon, Jefferies: Asked timing and what’s embedded in 2026 guidance for the $100M-$120M savings; CFO Jill Griebenow: "none of that has been baked into our 2026 guidance" for Canada/Australia-related savings, and for incremental actions, "we expect the 2026 savings... to be in that $20 million to $25 million range."

  • Benjamin Budish, Barclays: Asked capital priorities and use of cash; CFO Jill Griebenow: "we continue to be focused on organic investments" in areas including "financial and economic event market, tokenizing products" and "further extending our clearing services," while also expecting "share repurchases again, opportunistically" and evaluating the dividend.

  • Ashish Sabadra, RBC Capital Markets: Asked competition and pricing for event contracts; CEO Craig Donohue said, "It is clear that binary options that are based on a broad-based stock index are a securities-based products" and added, "we're very hopeful and optimistic that regulatory clarity ultimately will result in no impact to our licensed products"; Executive VP Robert Hocking said, "we have a fair amount of flexibility in being competitive with pricing" but "we haven't finalized the fee structure yet."

  • Michael Cyprys, Morgan Stanley: Asked clearing expansion and tokenization economics; CEO Craig Donohue said, "We see tremendous benefits in expanding our clearing capabilities" and referenced European clearing expansion "to include securities finance transactions," while adding, "I can't comment on pricing and economics at the moment" for tokenization.

  • Simon Clinch, Rothschild & Co Redburn: Asked sizing of event-contract opportunity vs funnel into core franchise; CEO Craig Donohue: "I think these markets can be huge" and described "an enormous multiplier effect" from "decomposing equity securities into all these different kind of event contracts."

Sentiment analysis

  • Analyst tone was slightly positive and opportunity-focused, with repeated probing on prediction markets, SPX defensibility, and how much cost savings are embedded.

  • Management tone was positive in prepared remarks and largely confident in Q&A, using forward-leaning language such as Donohue’s "we're super keen" and Griebenow’s "we are lowering our 2026 adjusted operating expense guidance," with limited direct answers on tokenization economics ("I can't comment on pricing and economics at the moment").

  • Versus the prior quarter, the discussion shifted from planning and early-stage positioning (Q4) to execution details and concrete actions (Q1), including explicit workforce and expense-guidance changes.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Guidance language tightened as Griebenow moved Data Vantage organic growth from "mid- to high single-digit" (Q4) to "low-double-digit" (Q1), and total organic net revenue growth from "mid-single-digit" (Q4) to "low-double-digit to mid-teens" (Q1).

  • Cost and restructuring became more explicit as Johnston announced actions "expected to reduce our workforce by approximately 20%" (Q1), while Griebenow lowered the 2026 adjusted operating expense range from $864 million-$879 million (Q4) to $838 million-$853 million (Q1).

  • Prediction markets advanced from targeting a launch timeline (Q4: "targeting a second quarter launch") to more detailed product framing in Q1, with Donohue emphasizing contracts that "mirror" a vertical spread and Hocking discussing KPI contracts and settlement design.

Risks and concerns

  • Donohue framed market sensitivity in SPX demand drivers, saying that in March "the macro outlook shifted abruptly with the Iran war" and investors shifted toward longer-dated hedging.

  • Event contracts depended on regulatory outcomes as Donohue said, "Subject to regulatory approval, we plan to bring our securities-based event contracts to market," and he referenced active discussions on competing index-based binaries as "an ongoing conversation that we're having" with regulators.

  • Execution risk around restructuring was highlighted by Johnston’s workforce reduction and shift "back to in-person work," and Griebenow’s comments that Canada/Australia sales remain subject to "separate closing and regulatory approval processes."

Final takeaway

Management portrayed Q1 as a record quarter while pairing strong results with a deeper strategic realignment, including a workforce reduction of about 20% and a lower 2026 adjusted operating expense guide of $838 million-$853 million. Leadership emphasized continued momentum in SPX and broader derivatives, accelerating Data Vantage growth expectations to low-double-digit organic growth, and expanding plans for securities-based event contracts and longer-term KPI-linked markets, while repeatedly noting that key product launches and divestitures remain subject to regulatory approval and timing.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: CNO outlines Medicare Supplement rate actions of 10.2% and ~14.5% approvals with full quarterly impact by Q4 2026
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: CNO Financial Group (CNO) Q1 2026

Management View

  • Operating earnings momentum and sales/distribution growth were the core themes in prepared remarks, with CEO Gary Bhojwani emphasizing, "CNO is off to a strong start to the year, building on our excellent 2025 performance," and adding that the company delivered "our 15th consecutive quarter of sales growth" and "our 13th consecutive quarter of producing agent count growth."
  • Bhojwani tied growth to product demand and distribution positioning, stating, "Sales results in the quarter were strong across both divisions with total new annualized premiums up 11%," and arguing, "Our exclusive middle market focus and our last mile captive agent distribution model create our durable competitive moat."
  • Consumer and Worksite product performance was positioned as broad-based, including Medicare-related strength, with Bhojwani stating, "Total Medicare policies sold were up 24% with Medicare Supplement NAP up 53%," and describing a market mix shift: "Our results continue to reflect the shift in consumer preferences away from Medicare Advantage and toward Medicare Supplement."
  • CFO Paul McDonough highlighted earnings, expenses, and capital deployment, saying, "Operating earnings per share were $1.05 for the quarter," and that the expense ratio was "18.9%, reflecting lower-than-planned spending in the quarter, which we expect to normalize over the balance of the year," while noting, "we deployed $60 million of excess capital on share repurchases."
  • On investments and capital strength, McDonough said, "Net investment income increased 6% year-over-year," and added, "Holding company liquidity ended the quarter at $280 million, well above our $150 million minimum target," while maintaining the RBC framework: "we manage between 360% and 390%."

Outlook

  • Management did not change its annual framework in the prepared remarks, with McDonough stating, "we are affirming our original guidance at this time," citing "the volatile macroeconomic environment" and adding, "we will refine our projections later in the year."
  • Long-term profitability ambition language strengthened, but without a new target this quarter, as McDonough said, "Our recent ROE results make it likely that we will increase our 2027 ROE ambitions," while also stating, "we don't believe it would be appropriate to update our '27 ROE target less than halfway through the 3-year cycle."

Financial Results

  • The quarter’s profitability and capital return were framed around operating EPS, capital return, and book value, with Bhojwani stating, "First quarter operating earnings per diluted share were up 33% to $1.05," adding, "We maintained a robust capital position while returning $77 million to shareholders," and reporting, "Book value per diluted share, excluding AOCI, was $38.98, up 5%."
  • McDonough attributed earnings drivers to underwriting, investments, and expense discipline, stating the increase "reflects continued profitable sales growth, strength in underwriting results, growth in net investment income driven by growth in assets, together with higher yields, and ongoing discipline in expense and capital management."
  • Product margin commentary emphasized diversification and specific pressure points, including, "Medicare Supplement faced modestly adverse claims experience," and an action plan: "We expect rate increases over the course of 2026 to help address the recent claims experience."
  • Investment income growth was linked to balance sheet expansion and reinvestment rates, with McDonough citing "growth in net insurance liabilities and related assets, which increased 4.8% in the quarter," and "13 consecutive quarters of new money rates above 6%," while noting, "alternative investment income improved over the prior year but was slightly below expectations."

Q&A

  • Suneet Kamath, Jefferies: Asked for Medicare Supplement pricing timing; CFO Paul McDonough responded, "in '25, we filed for rate increases" including "an increase of 10.5%, and we received approvals for 10.2%" and another filing of "16.8%, and we're expecting approvals for around 14.5%," adding, "The full quarterly impact over these 2 blocks [is] evident by fourth quarter of this year."
  • Suneet Kamath, Jefferies: Asked block sizing; McDonough said, "The closed block... is maybe 2/3" and "the open block, about 1/3 roughly."
  • Suneet Kamath, Jefferies: Pressed on Consumer product mix (strong Health NAP vs. softer life/D2C/annuities); CEO Gary Bhojwani said, "I didn't see any particular anomalies in the first quarter," adding, "to be really blunt, I don't pay that much attention to volume from quarter-to-quarter," and concluding, "I am extremely bullish."
  • Ryan Krueger, KBW: Asked whether expense favorability was timing vs. structural; McDonough said, "we expect that the expenses for the full year will still come in around where we had originally planned," while noting, "the growth we're seeing in the business will drive likely some favorability in the denominator of the ratio."
  • Ryan Krueger, KBW: Asked about credit markets and deployment opportunities; CIO Eric Johnson said credit spreads "were actually pretty flat," and added, "new money rate... is around a little higher than 6%," while cautioning, "Don't think this story is in the ninth inning."
  • Joel Hurwitz, Dowling: Asked what’s driving ROE improvement sustainability; McDonough replied, "it's really a combination of things... across the value chain," including "optimize capital."
  • Joel Hurwitz, Dowling: Asked if long-term care margin is a “new normal”; McDonough said, "long-term care continues to perform exceptionally well" and "continues to surprise us a bit to the upside."
  • Francis Matten, BMO: Asked about ROE offsets/normalization; McDonough warned, "nonoperating income... is volatile," and Bhojwani reiterated, "12% is nothing more than a way point."
  • Francis Matten, BMO: Asked RBC sensitivity to equity markets/hedges; McDonough answered, "yes and yes," explaining the quarter’s impact from "the S&P being down about 5%," and added, "as the equity markets recover... that unwinds."
  • Wilma Burdis, Raymond James: Asked about FABN spread environment; Johnson said a Q1 offering "would have perhaps produced a marginal contribution," and added the company’s next window "will probably be in June," stressing, "we don't have to issue unless the time is right for us."
  • Wilma Burdis, Raymond James: Asked about mortality expectations; McDonough said results were "within the normal range of expectations" and added, "we'll... revisit our assumptions in the third quarter."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were neutral-to-slightly cautious, focusing on claim pressure, expense sustainability, market volatility, capital sensitivity, and funding spreads, including questions on "Med Supp claims," whether expenses were "all timing related," and whether FABN spreads were "less favorable."
  • Management tone in prepared remarks was positive and confident, with Bhojwani saying, "CNO is off to a strong start," while responses in Q&A stayed measured on pressure points, including McDonough’s emphasis that claims would be addressed through filings and that equity-driven RBC noise "unwinds."
  • Compared with last quarter, management repeated the same confidence framing around ROE ("way point") while shifting from initiating 2026 guidance (Q4) to "affirming" it (Q1) and deferring any 2027 target increase until "no later than early next year."

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Guidance posture shifted from establishing 2026 ranges in Q4 to holding them steady in Q1, with McDonough now saying, "we are affirming our original guidance at this time" versus Q4 guidance of "operating earnings per share between $4.25 and $4.45" and expense ratio guidance of "18.8% to 19.2%."
  • Medicare Supplement remained a demand tailwind in both calls, but Q1 introduced more explicit claims/remediation detail, including McDonough’s disclosure of rate filings/approvals and timing to Q4 impact, whereas Q4 emphasized sales momentum and market shift dynamics.
  • Capital-market sensitivity was discussed more specifically in Q1, with the RBC discussion tied to "the S&P being down about 5%" and subsequent unwind, while Q4 centered more on capital deployment philosophy and investment positioning.

Risks and Concerns

  • Medical claims pressure in Medicare Supplement was acknowledged directly, with McDonough stating, "Medicare Supplement faced modestly adverse claims experience," and mitigation centered on approved and pending rate actions that "earn in over time."
  • Macroeconomic and market volatility remained a gating factor for guidance updates, with McDonough citing "the volatile macroeconomic environment," and highlighting statutory reserve/RBC noise tied to equity moves that "unwinds" as markets recover.
  • Funding-spread conditions may constrain FABN issuance timing, with Johnson saying Q1 conditions could have made issuance only "a marginal contribution," adding, "we're keeping a close eye on it" and "under no pressure to do anything."

Final Takeaway

Management characterized Q1 2026 as a continuation of multi-year momentum—operating EPS of $1.05, sales growth across both divisions, and continued net investment income expansion—while flagging specific pressure points in Medicare Supplement claims and near-term market-driven RBC noise. The company said it is affirming its original 2026 guidance due to macro volatility, outlined a defined Med Supp rate-increase cadence expected to show full quarterly impact by Q4 2026, and reiterated that 12% ROE is “nothing more than a way point,” with any updated 2027 ambition to be communicated no later than early next year.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: First Solar rises as Q1 earnings top consensus despite tariffs
data.description:
First Solar (FSLR) up 6.3% to its highest level since February after reporting Q1 earnings that beat analyst estimates on improving margins, offsetting some tariff impacts, and reaching a record $1.04B in revenues for any Q1.

Q1 net income rose to $347M, or $3.22/share, from $210M, or $1.95/share, and adjusted EBITDA jumped to $520M from $379M in the year-earlier quarter, as net sales jumped 24% Y/Y.

Q1 gross margins jumped to 47% from 41% in the year-earlier quarter, underpinned by a 31% increase in module volumes sold to third parties to 3.8 GW, alongside $418M in Section 45X tax credits, and the company's module sales backlog totaled 47.9 GW, or ~$14.4B, through 2030.

For the full year, First Solar (FSLR) reaffirmed guidance for revenues of $4.9B-$5.2B, in line with the $5.13B FactSet analyst consensus estimate, volume sold at 17.0-18.2 GW, and adjusted EBITDA of $2.6B-$2.8B.

For Q2, the company forecast module sales of 3.4-4.0 GW and Section 45X tax credits of $330M-$400M, which are expected to result in Q2 adjusted EBITDA of $400M-$500M.

First Solar's (FSLR) Q1 profit beat as margins improved came despite a challenging environment for solar demand, William Blair analyst Jed Dorsheimer said, noting the company has been pressured by increasing underutilization charges in Malaysia and Vietnam as well as a 15% flat tariff and Section 232 tariffs on steel and aluminum.

data.title: PGE reiterates $3.33-$3.53 2026 EPS guidance as it targets mid-2027 Washington close
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Portland General Electric (POR) Q1 2026

Management view

  • "The first quarter delivered another stretch of warm winter weather, 10% year-over-year Industrial customer demand growth and continued maturity of our cost management initiatives," said President, CEO & Director Maria Pope.

  • "For the first quarter, we reported GAAP net income of $45 million or $0.38 per diluted share and non-GAAP net income of $68 million or $0.58 per share," said President, CEO & Director Pope.

  • "We will be engaging with our regulator to explore frameworks to help mitigate weather and other volatility impacting both revenue and power costs," said President, CEO & Director Pope, adding, "Greater predictability is good for both customers and shareholders, and we recognize that this will be multiyear work."

  • "We are reiterating our full year earnings guidance of $3.33 to $3.53 per diluted share and our long-term earnings and dividend growth guidance of 5% to 7%," said President, CEO & Director Pope.

  • On the Washington acquisition process, President, CEO & Director Pope said, "We anticipate the regulatory approval process to take about a year and continue to target a mid-2027 close."

  • On the holding company docket, President, CEO & Director Pope said, "To prioritize timely resolution of the holding company, we have paused the transmission company," and added, "That said, formation of a transmission company remains part of our long-term strategy."

  • On the large load tariff filing, President, CEO & Director Pope said, "Our proposed rate structure under consideration enabled by Oregon's recent legislation includes a 26% increase in data center prices, which will help reduce the cost borne by Residential and Small Business customers."

  • "We foresee robust energy usage from data centers and high-tech customers with large customer capacity growing by about 10% compounded annually through 2030," said President, CEO & Director Pope.

  • "Total Q1 2026 loads were flattish compared to Q1 2025," said Senior VP of Finance & CFO Joseph Trpik, adding, "Industrial demand increased 10% on a nominal and weather-adjusted basis."

  • "In the first quarter, we executed a $550 million equity forward to address our 2026 base equity needs and fund the 2023 RFP project," said Senior VP of Finance & CFO Trpik.

Outlook

  • "After considering the recent trends in customer usage, we now anticipate weather-adjusted load growth of 1.5% to 2.5% this year," said Senior VP of Finance & CFO Trpik.

  • "Relative to our plan, Q1 was $0.25 below our expectations," said Senior VP of Finance & CFO Trpik. "While $0.09 is driven by timing, we will address the remainder through refining our capital and maintenance work streams, optimizing our team, equipment and facilities management and positioning our power portfolio and generation fleet to deliver optimal value," he added.

  • "We are confident that these cost savings measures are achievable," said Senior VP of Finance & CFO Trpik, citing "the $25 million we saved last year" and "the opportunity to accelerate what was planned for 2027 into this year."

  • "We are reaffirming our long-term earnings and dividend growth guidance of 5% to 7% and our full year adjusted earnings guidance of $3.33 to $3.53 per diluted share," said Senior VP of Finance & CFO Trpik.

Financial results

  • Q1 non-GAAP EPS of $0.58 vs. analysts’ non-GAAP EPS estimate of $0.77; Q1 GAAP EPS was $0.38.

  • "Commercial load decreased 2.9% or 2.3% weather-adjusted and Residential load decreased 6.2% or 4.6% weather adjusted," said Senior VP of Finance & CFO Trpik.

  • "We experienced a $0.07 increase in retail revenues," said Senior VP of Finance & CFO Trpik, including "a $0.09 increase from additional cost recovery, largely from the inclusion of our Seaside battery asset in customer rates beginning in November 2025." He also cited "a decrease from power cost of $0.15" and "a $0.16 decrease from other capital and financing costs" as quarter-over-quarter earnings drivers.

  • "Total liquidity at the end of the quarter was $954 million," said Senior VP of Finance & CFO Trpik, adding, "Our investment-grade credit ratings remain unchanged" and "with an estimated 2026 CFO to debt metric above 19%."

  • "This quarter, we also entered into 2 unsecured credit agreements," said Senior VP of Finance & CFO Trpik, including "a $350 million term loan facility maturing in March 2028" and "a $680 million delayed draw term loan intended to finance the Washington acquisition-related costs."

  • "In April, the Board of Directors declared a quarterly common stock dividend of $0.5512 per share, representing an increase of 5% on an annualized basis," said Senior VP of Finance & CFO Trpik.

Q&A

  • Julien Dumoulin-Smith, Jefferies: Asked about holding company sticking points and the impact of pausing TransCo; President, CEO & Director Maria Pope answered, "We've agreed upon some bunch of general provisions around ring-fencing..." but "we still remain pretty far apart with regards to credit, the use of leverage and other such things."

  • Julien Dumoulin-Smith, Jefferies: Asked about levers to support reaffirmed guidance; Senior VP of Finance & CFO Joseph Trpik answered, "we look to our toolkit" and described a "two-pronged" approach of "adapt our energy portfolio" and "adapt to our cost."

  • Whitney Mutalemwa, Wells Fargo: Asked about recovery tools after RCE and multiyear rate planning; President, CEO & Director Pope answered, "We are engaging with regulators to work in removing the volatility and generating more predictability," and added, "we have a lot of work to do around common understanding of how we'll attract and retain capital."

  • Christopher Ellinghaus, Siebert Williams Shank: Asked about Oregon economy, customer growth, and revised load expectations; President, CEO & Director Pope answered, "We consider customer growth to actually continue to be quite strong," while Senior VP of Finance & CFO Trpik attributed patterns to "an unusual set of weather patterns" and said the remainder of the year "overall net should be relatively close to 0."

  • Aidan Kelly, JPMorgan: Asked about Washington acquisition milestones and next rate case timing; President, CEO & Director Pope said stakeholder outreach "could not have gone better" and added, "we have not decided exactly on the next timing of our rate case... it will probably be sometime in the second half of the year."

  • Gregg Orrill, UBS: Asked when 2025 RFP could enter CapEx plans; Senior VP of Finance & CFO Trpik said, "the earliest we'll start to see contracts is the beginning of 2027."

  • Paul Fremont, Ladenburg Thalmann: Pressed on HoldCo settlement timing and Washington returns; President, CEO & Director Pope said, "we still have good expectations of being able to settle," and on Washington said, "we expect it to be accretive in the first year" and "drive to a similar return profile in Washington as we have in Oregon or better."

  • Travis Miller, Morningstar: Asked about the 26% data center price increase scope and owned vs. purchased generation mix; President, CEO & Director Pope said, "they were for existing and new customers, all data center customers," and Senior VP of Finance & CFO Trpik said, "there's no strategic changes going on there."

  • Rue Jia, Mizuho: Asked what a growth-oriented approach looks like post-acquisition; Senior VP of Finance & CFO Trpik said near term it is "supporting and giving them the right investment, mainly on the D side" with longer-term growth tied to "RFPs" and economic development.

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts: Slightly negative to neutral, with repeated pressure on regulatory construct and cost recovery tools (e.g., Wells Fargo’s question: "with the RCE mechanism no longer available, how are you thinking about the path to future reliability-related costs...").

  • Management: Slightly positive in prepared remarks with reaffirmed guidance (President, CEO & Director Pope: "we are reiterating our full year earnings guidance"), and more guarded on regulatory friction in Q&A (President, CEO & Director Pope: "we still remain pretty far apart with regards to credit, the use of leverage").

  • Versus last quarter: The Q1 call carried more emphasis on weather-driven volatility and mitigation frameworks (President, CEO & Director Pope: "multiyear work"), while the prior call’s tone centered on deal announcement and accretion framing.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • The Washington acquisition moved from announcement to filing stage; in Q1, President, CEO & Director Pope said, "we filed applications..." and "continue to target a mid-2027 close," compared with Q4’s "definitive agreement" and expectation the process would take "approximately 12 months after regulatory filings are submitted."

  • Data center pricing language shifted from "a 25% price increase" in Q4 to "a 26% increase in data center prices" in Q1, and Q1 clarified in Q&A that the increase applies to "existing and new customers."

  • Load outlook was reduced; Q4 guided "2026 weather-adjusted load growth guidance of 2.5% to 3.5%," while Q1 said, "we now anticipate weather-adjusted load growth of 1.5% to 2.5% this year."

  • Q1 highlighted a near-term earnings gap to plan and cost offsets (Senior VP of Finance & CFO Trpik: "Q1 was $0.25 below our expectations"), a topic not framed as such in Q4.

Risks and concerns

  • "Extremely mild weather" and shifting residential/small commercial usage were identified as drivers of volatility (President, CEO & Director Pope: "Our results reflect extremely mild weather"; Senior VP of Finance & CFO Trpik: "seasonal shifts... with rooftop solar adoption and energy efficiency growth").

  • Regulatory uncertainty around structural filings was explicit (President, CEO & Director Pope: "we still remain pretty far apart with regards to credit"), alongside the decision to "pause" the transmission company to focus on HoldCo.

  • Exposure to wholesale and environmental credit markets was cited (Senior VP of Finance & CFO Trpik: power cost performance was "driven by less favorable wholesale and environmental credit market conditions").

Final takeaway

Management reaffirmed full-year adjusted EPS guidance of $3.33 to $3.53 and long-term EPS and dividend growth of 5% to 7% while describing Q1 as below plan due to mild weather and lower residential demand. The company emphasized accelerating cost management actions to offset the gap, advancing regulatory filings for the Washington acquisition with a mid-2027 close target, and pushing a large load tariff that includes a 26% increase in data center prices intended to improve affordability for other customer classes, alongside continued efforts to pursue regulatory frameworks that reduce weather and power-cost volatility.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: GrafTech expects 5% to 10% 2026 sales volume growth as it rolls out $600 to $1,200 per ton price increase
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: GrafTech International Ltd. (EAF) Q1 2026

Management view

  • “On March 26, we announced that we're increasing our graphite electrode prices by a minimum of $600 to $1,200 per metric ton, depending on the region,” said CEO Timothy Flanagan, adding, “This increase will only apply to volume that was not yet committed as of that date... As we remain focused on value over volume, we'll continue to walk away from volume opportunities that do not meet our margin requirements.”
  • Flanagan tied pricing to trade enforcement and customer behavior: “In April, the International Trade Commission announced the preliminary determination that there is a reasonable indication that the domestic industry is being materially injured by imports from China and India,” and said, “We are beginning to see a shift in sourcing behavior for certain steel producers with an increased focus on regional production and surety of supply.”
  • On operations and demand signals, Flanagan said, “we are starting to see signs of improvement,” citing U.S. utilization: “the AISI reporting that weekly U.S. capacity utilization rate at 80% for just the second time in the past 2 years,” while noting Europe remained weaker: “in the EU, steel output for the first quarter remained depressed, declining 3% compared to the prior year.”
  • CFO Rory O'Donnell reported volume/mix progress and reiterated the company’s commercial stance: “our sales volume in the first quarter was 28,000 metric tons, an increase of 14% compared to the prior year,” and “we delivered 37% sales volume growth year-over-year in the U.S. for the first quarter.”
  • O'Donnell detailed the quarter’s price/cost positioning: “Our average selling price for the first quarter was approximately $3,900 per metric ton,” and “For the first quarter, our cash costs on a per metric ton basis were $3,848... a 4% sequential decline from the fourth quarter,” adding, “we are pleased with our ongoing progress towards achieving our long-term expectation of cash costs being approximately $3,600 to $3,700 per metric ton.”

Outlook

  • “For the full year, we remain on track to achieve our original guidance of a 5% to 10% year-over-year increase in total sales volume,” said CFO Rory O'Donnell.
  • CEO Timothy Flanagan said the company is “maintaining our cost guidance for a low single-digit improvement over 2025,” while warning, “the extended duration of the conflict in the Middle East and the resulting longer-term impact on the oil and energy markets remains uncertain,” and, “Sustained increases in our key input costs will require us to take further action on electrode pricing.”
  • On price timing, O'Donnell said, “probably 90% of the volume that will be impacted by the price increase will happen in the second half of the year,” adding, “I wouldn't expect to have a big change in ASP in the second quarter, but would really see that start to materialize in the third and fourth quarter.”

Financial results

  • CFO Rory O'Donnell said, “For the first quarter, we had a net loss of $43 million or $1.66 per share,” and “Adjusted EBITDA was negative $14 million... primarily due to the decline in our average price.”
  • On cash flow and spending, O'Donnell said, “cash used in operating activities was $15 million,” “Adjusted free cash flow was negative $27 million,” and “we continue to anticipate a full year spend will be approximately $35 million.”
  • On liquidity and near-term funding actions, O'Donnell said, “We ended the first quarter with total liquidity of $329 million,” and, “our expectation remains to draw on this residual portion, most likely by the end of the second quarter,” referring to the delayed draw term loan availability.

Q&A

  • Bennett Moore, JPMorgan: asked about “what sort of inflation you're seeing from decant oil... [and] upward pressure on needle coke”; CEO Timothy Flanagan: “decant oil as a percentage of our total production cost is around 25%,” and “we have fixed price contracts going through the end of the year” for EU energy.
  • Bennett Moore, JPMorgan: asked if pricing “could be a trough” and when it inflects; CFO Rory O'Donnell: “I wouldn't expect to have a big change in ASP in the second quarter... [it] start to materialize in the third and fourth quarter.”
  • Arun Viswanathan, RBC: pressed on applying the announced price hike to results and “the path to profitability”; CEO Timothy Flanagan: “you can't just apply it to all the tons... You can only apply it to the incremental tons,” and said the move is about “positioning for that next major round... as we head into '27.”
  • Abraham Landa, BofA: asked for cost exposure beyond decant oil; CFO Rory O'Donnell: “Those 2 together are about 10% to 15%,” referring to electricity and natural gas, and said EU power/gas are largely covered by “fixed price contracts.”
  • Kirk Ludtke, Imperial Capital: asked about trade-case timing and 2027 negotiations; CEO Timothy Flanagan: “the Countervailing duties ruling could be implied or applied no later than the end of July... antidumping... mid-September,” and said preliminary margins were “74% against Indian imports” and “147% against Chinese imports,” adding, “Roughly” when asked if the countries represented “20% of the U.S. market.”

Sentiment analysis

  • “Skeptical, pressing” analyst tone centered on cost inflation, timing/efficacy of price increases, and trade-case outcomes, including questions like “do you think 1Q could be a trough for the year?” (Bennett Moore, JPMorgan).
  • “Slightly positive” management tone on price momentum and trade/policy catalysts, with caution on geopolitics: “we're encouraged by our customers' reaction” (CEO Timothy Flanagan) alongside “remains uncertain” (Flanagan) on oil/energy impacts.
  • Compared with last quarter, management messaging shifted from “one of the most challenging environments” and expecting pressure “to continue into 2026” (Q4 2025) toward “signs of improvement” and “positive pricing momentum” (Q1 2026), while analysts remained focused on whether pricing recovery will translate into reported results.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Current-quarter emphasis moved from Q4 2025’s broad “evaluation of a number of areas, including optimizing our manufacturing footprint... strategic partnerships and sources of capital” (CEO Timothy Flanagan) to Q1 2026’s concrete commercial action: “increasing our graphite electrode prices by a minimum of $600 to $1,200 per metric ton” (Flanagan).
  • Order-book visibility improved: Q4 2025 said “approximately 65% committed” (CFO Rory O'Donnell) versus Q1 2026 “more than 85%... committed” (CEO Timothy Flanagan).
  • Liquidity was described as $340 million at Q4 2025 year-end (CFO Rory O'Donnell) versus $329 million at Q1 2026 quarter-end (O'Donnell), with continued intent to draw the remaining delayed draw term loan by end of Q2 2026.

Risks and concerns

  • CEO Timothy Flanagan warned of geopolitics-driven cost risk: “Disruptions in the production and transportation of oil out of the Middle East are having a significant impact on the global oil market... translated into higher decant oil prices.”
  • CFO Rory O'Donnell added potential merchant-market tightening: “as we get into the second half of the year, you'll see a marked increase in needle coke prices on the merchant side,” while stressing mitigation from vertical integration.
  • Management flagged pricing/volume discipline risk: “we'll continue to walk away from volume opportunities that do not meet our margin requirements” (Flanagan).

Final takeaway

Management framed Q1 2026 as an early turning point driven by a newly announced $600 to $1,200 per metric ton electrode price increase, improving U.S. steel utilization signals, and advancing U.S. trade-case milestones expected in July and September. The company reiterated 2026 volume growth guidance of 5% to 10%, maintained expectations for a low single-digit cash-cost improvement versus 2025, and pointed to liquidity of $329 million while acknowledging that prolonged Middle East-driven oil and energy volatility could require additional pricing actions.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
